questions.prompt: Which TMS sequence reliably drives a standard JTAG TAP to Test-Logic-Reset from an unknown state?

choices.A: Hold TMS low for one clock
choices.B: Hold TMS high for at least five TCK edges

questions.explanation: From every TAP state, five consecutive TMS-high transitions reach Test-Logic-Reset. This gives the host a state-independent recovery sequence.

questions.prompt: When does a value shifted through a JTAG instruction register become the active instruction?
questions.explanation: Capture, shift, and update are distinct phases. The shift register can change bit by bit without affecting the active instruction until Update-IR.

questions.prompt: Before connecting a programmer to an unknown target, which evidence is most important?

choices.A: The color of the PCB solder mask
choices.B: Verified common ground, connector orientation, signal voltage, and drive ownership

questions.explanation: Mechanical fit does not establish electrical compatibility. Wrong voltage, orientation, ground, or two active drivers can damage both target and adapter.

questions.prompt: What is the safest first-power practice for an unverified board?

choices.A: Use the highest available current limit so it boots quickly
choices.B: Use a current-limited supply, known ground, measured checkpoints, and stop on excess current or heat
choices.C: Program the largest bitstream before measuring rails

questions.explanation: A conservative current limit and staged measurements contain faults. Excess current, heat, odor, or unstable rails are stop conditions, not prompts to continue.

questions.prompt: A board has an unproven core rail, failed JTAG, and garbled UART. Which should be investigated first?

choices.C: The core rail and other lower-level power/clock/reset prerequisites

questions.explanation: Higher-layer symptoms are uninterpretable until their dependencies are proven. Bringup proceeds from safe power through clock/reset and debug before application interfaces.
